hook: Colossal Nazi U-boat bunker turned digital-art cathedral
facts: Built 1941-43 by some 6,500 forced and conscripted workers, many Spanish Republican exiles.
Concrete roof up to several metres thick; Allied bombing barely scratched it.
Eleven pens linked by an internal street, sized for fifteen submarines.
Since 2020 houses the Bassins des Lumieres immersive art centre.
w: The Nazis poured metres of concrete over their U-boat pens here, raised by thousands of forced labourers; Allied bombs barely chipped it. Too vast to demolish, the city handed the bunker to artists, who flood eleven submarine alveoli with projected light. Brutality, repurposed.

hook: Squatted cavalry barracks reborn as an eco-skatepark and mural sprawl
facts: Occupies the 19th-century Caserne Niel, abandoned by the army, reclaimed from 2007.
Indoor Hangar skatepark draws skaters from across the region.
Walls painted by graffiti names including JonOne and Jeff Soto.
Now hosts hundreds of ecological start-ups, co-working desks and an organic canteen.
w: Cavalry once drilled in these riverside barracks; the army left and squatters moved in, dragging ramps into the gun-grey halls. Now skaters carve under murals by JonOne and Jeff Soto while eco start-ups type away next door. Drilled for war, repainted for the future.

hook: Gothic spire with a crypt that once displayed natural mummies
facts: Freestanding 114-metre bell tower, the second tallest in France.
A crypt at its base long served as a parish ossuary.
Naturally preserved corpses, found when the cemetery was cleared, were posed and labelled as mummies.
Hugo, Flaubert and Gautier all came to gawp; the display closed in 1979.
legend: Placards supposedly named 'the family poisoned by mushrooms' and 'the one buried alive'.
w: Beneath this freestanding Gothic spire, gravediggers clearing the old churchyard found bodies the dry soil had mummified. Stood in a ring and labelled, supposedly, 'poisoned by mushrooms' or 'buried alive', they drew Hugo and Flaubert to stare. The macabre circle closed only in 1979.

hook: Ruined Roman amphitheatre stranded among bourgeois townhouses
facts: Built around the 2nd century, the largest Gallo-Roman structure in the region.
Once held tens of thousands; only a wall of arches survives.
Used as a quarry and a refuge for outcasts before being saved.
Now boxed in on every side by 18th-century houses.
legend: Demonologist Pierre de Lancre recorded a belief that witches held their sabbath in the ruins.
w: Turn off a quiet residential street and a wall of Roman arches looms between the townhouses, all that's left of an amphitheatre that once roared. Later a quarry, a thieves' den, and, legend says, a place where witches met for their sabbath. A coliseum gone feral in the suburbs.

hook: Underground Roman cemetery of stacked stone sarcophagi
facts: Excavated under the basilica square in 1909-10 by Camille Jullian.
Around 70 limestone and marble sarcophagi now visible in the crypt.
More than a thousand more are thought to lie under the surrounding streets.
Burials span the 4th to 13th centuries; a UNESCO pilgrimage-route site.
legend: Said to hold relics tied to Charlemagne's paladin Roland of Roncevaux.
w: Dig under the basilica square and you hit the dead, layer on layer, from the late Romans to the Middle Ages. Some seventy stone coffins stand visible in the crypt; a thousand more wait under the neighbourhood. Legend even ties a relic here to Roland of Roncevaux. The city built on its graveyard.

hook: Arthouse screens inside a deconsecrated medieval church
facts: Set in the 13th-century Saint-Simeon church, a cemetery until 1489.
After the Revolution it served as a warehouse, naval school and sardine cannery.
Reopened as an independent cinema in 1999.
Five baroque-walled screens plus a bar under the old beams.
w: Saint-Simeon's church kept its dead, then went through revolution as a warehouse, a sailors' school, even a sardine cannery. Since 1999 you watch films under its vaults instead, five screens carved into the old stone. Worship the moving image where they once buried the parish.

hook: Medieval belfry-gate that rang the curfew and jailed children
facts: Twin-towered gate built from the 13th century on Roman foundations.
Served as town belfry, prison and one of the city's old gates.
Its bell, Armande-Louise, cast in 1775, weighs nearly eight tonnes.
A golden leopard weathervane crowns the central tower.
w: Twin pepper-pot towers straddle the old Saint-Eloi street, raised on Roman footings to ring the curfew and the harvest. Its huge bell, Armande-Louise, still booms on rare days. Above the arch a tiny prison once held young offenders, who learned the hours by the toll over their heads.

hook: Fairytale gate built to flatter a victorious king
facts: Raised 1493-96 as a triumphal gate honouring Charles VIII.
Marks his win at the 1495 Battle of Fornovo in Italy.
Mixes real defences, machicolations and loopholes, with fanciful conical roofs.
Climb 35 metres for a balcony over the Garonne.
w: Half fortress, half stage set, this riverside gate went up in the 1490s to flatter Charles VIII after his Italian campaign. The machicolations are real enough; the witch-hat turrets and royal niche are pure pageant. Climb its 35 metres for a balcony over the muddy Garonne.

hook: 18th-century folly hung with a wine baron's contemporary art
facts: Set in the neoclassical Chateau Labottiere, a listed 18th-century townhouse.
Bought and restored in 2011 by wine magnate Bernard Magrez.
Holds the largest private contemporary collection in Bordeaux, some 550 works.
French formal garden also classified as a historic monument.
w: Two printer brothers built this prim neoclassical folly in the 1700s, all pediments and box hedges. A wine magnate bought it, restored it, and hung the rooms with edgy contemporary work, hundreds of pieces, his private hoard. Old money's mansion, crashed by living art.

hook: Paved promenade of the wine-merchant aristocracy
facts: Laid out around 1750 by intendant Tourny, nicknamed the Pave des Chartrons.
Lined with mansions of English, Irish and Flemish wine negociants.
Their trade earned the mocking title 'aristocracy of the cork'.
Renamed in 1932 for a Bordeaux medical professor.
w: Foreign wine merchants, English, Irish, Flemish, built their stone mansions along this paved walk Tourny cut around 1750, the old Pave des Chartrons. Locals sneered at the 'aristocracy of the cork'; the balusters and pediments don't care. Faded grandeur, distilled from claret money.

hook: The belly of Bordeaux, where shuckers open oysters at dawn
facts: First market here in 1749, prompted by the Marquis de Tourny.
Iron-and-glass halls roofed the stalls from 1881.
Locals call it 'le ventre de Bordeaux', the city's belly.
Arcachon oysters opened to order with a glass of white wine.
w: They call it the belly of Bordeaux, and on a Sunday morning it heaves: knives flashing over Arcachon oysters, a glass of cold white pressed into your hand before nine. Iron and glass since 1881, the stalls a babel of cheese, blood and brine. Come hungry; leave wobbly.

hook: A vaulted shopping arcade financed by a shipowning baron
facts: Cut through by Baron Sarget, a wine trader and shipowner who paid for it.
Links the Cours de l'Intendance to Place du Chapelet.
A late example, contemporary with Milan's Galleria of 1877.
Bought by the city in 1919 as a safer pedestrian route.
w: A wine baron and shipowner drove this glass-roofed cut between two streets when Paris was already tearing its arcades down for department stores. Bordeaux kept its; the city even bought it to spare walkers a road thought too rough. A pocket of 1880s elegance, ducked out of the rain.

hook: An Art Deco 'palace of labour' for the city's unions
facts: Built 1935-38 by city architect Jacques d'Welles for the trade unions.
Reinforced concrete frame housing a congress hall and union rooms.
Facade relief by Alfred Janniot, sculptor of the Porte Doree in Paris.
Interior frescoes by Beaux-Arts artists glorify port, labour and peace.
w: Conceived in the 1930s as a palace for working people, this slab of Art Deco concrete gave the unions a congress hall and a grand stair. Janniot, who carved Paris's Porte Doree, cut the facade relief; inside, frescoes glorify the port and honest toil. Monumental dignity, for the proletariat.

hook: Napoleon's seventeen-arched bridge and its persistent myth
facts: First permanent bridge over the Garonne here, built 1819-22.
Ordered by Napoleon in 1808, finished long after his fall.
Seventeen arches span 486 metres across a fierce tidal river.
Brick medallions on the piers honour the emperor.
legend: Locals claim the seventeen arches match the letters in 'Napoleon Bonaparte'; the archives say no.
w: Napoleon demanded a bridge across the tidal Garonne; it opened in 1822, years after his fall. Seventeen arches, and locals swear that's the letter-count of 'Napoleon Bonaparte'. Engineers shrug: that's just what the vicious current needed. Either way, the river finally got its crossing.

hook: Burdigala's lost Roman temple, and a 27,000-year-old goddess
facts: Some 70,000 objects tracing the region from prehistory onward.
Holds the carved Venus a la Corne from Laussel, about 27,000 years old.
Displays fragments of the Piliers de Tutelle, a Roman temple razed in 1677.
A frank section confronts Bordeaux's role in the slave trade.
w: Inside hangs a woman carved 27,000 years ago, the Venus a la Corne, raising her horn. Nearby lie chunks of the Piliers de Tutelle, the Roman temple Louis XIV's men tore down in 1677. The museum also stares hard at the city's slaving fortunes. Burdigala, reassembled and reckoned with.

hook: A film of water that flips the 18th-century facade upside down
facts: Laid in 2006 facing the Place de la Bourse by Tourny and the Gabriels.
World's largest reflecting pool, around 3,450 square metres.
A two-centimetre film alternates with a drifting artificial fog.
Granite slab mirrors the royal square's blond stone.
w: The grand royal square went up in the 1700s to face the river; in 2006 someone laid a granite sheet before it and ran two centimetres of water over the stone. The classical facade drops headfirst into the puddle, then dissolves into drifting fog. Children shriek; the architecture, doubled.

hook: A bell tower built apart so its peals wouldn't shake the cathedral
facts: Raised 1440-1500 beside Saint-Andre cathedral, deliberately freestanding.
Detached to spare the church marshy foundations from bell vibration.
Crowned in 1863 by a gilded statue of Notre-Dame d'Aquitaine.
233 steps lead to a panorama over the rooftops.
w: Bishop Pey Berland set his belfry apart from the cathedral on purpose: the soft, marshy ground couldn't take the shudder of heavy bells against the nave. So it stands alone, a golden virgin added in 1863 glinting on top. Climb 233 steps for the city laid flat below.
